info.topictitle: A3.2.5 Stress, strain and material failure
info.parenttitle: A3.2 Introduction to structural systems (HL only)

markdown:
## Stress and Strain


<callout type="note">**Stress** and **strain** are fundamental concepts that describe how materials respond to external forces.</callout>

#### Stress


<definition term="Stress">A **force applied** to a material **divided by its cross-sectional area**. Measured in **Pascals (Pa).**</definition>

<callout type="note">**Formula**:

Stress = Force ÷ Area

$$σ = \frac{F}{A}$$

* **σ** = stress (in Pascals, **Pa**)
* **F** = force applied (in **Newtons**, N)
* **A** = cross-sectional area (in **square metres**, m²)</callout>

![FEA stress analysis of a part (simscale)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/69149103088ca4c358d4e002a59abfbd6bd624fc-1920x1080.png)

#### Strain


<definition term="Strain">The **change in length** of a material **divided by its original length**, caused by **stress**.</definition>

<callout type="note">**Formula**:

Strain = Change in Length ÷ Original Length

$$ε = \frac{ΔL}{  L}$$

* **ε** = strain (no units – it's a ratio)
* **ΔL** = change in length (in **metres**, m)
* **L** = original length (in **metres**, m)</callout>

<callout type="note">Strain is a **ratio**, meaning it has no units. It simply describes how much a material has **stretched **or **compressed**.</callout>

![The elastic band stretches when force is applied, showing change in length relative to its original size.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b2eb5a1b95b0ab7e7ebd5f2dde747dcb10156df6-433x360.png)

## The Stress-Strain Relationship


<definition term="Stress-strain graph">A **visual representation** of how a material **responds to a load**.</definition>

![Stress-strain graph.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1de7cef0064989a99e4ea601451bd8938e6dd1c5-3522x2601.png)

<callout type="note">* **Elastic deformation**: The material **returns to its original shape** when the force is removed.
* **Plastic deformation**: The material is **permanently stretched or bent** and does **not return** to its original shape.</callout>

### Key Points on a Stress-Strain Graph


#### 1. Young's Modulus


<definition term="Young's Modulus">A measure of a material’s **stiffness**, defined as **stress divided by strain**.</definition>

<callout type="tip">A **steeper **slope indicates a **stiffer **material, while a **gentler **slope indicates a more **flexible **material.</callout>

#### 2. Yield Strength


<definition term="Yield Strength ">The **maximum stress** a material can handle before **plastic deformation** begins.</definition>

<callout type="note">Beyond this point, the material will not return to its original shape when the load is removed.</callout>

#### 3. Ultimate Strength


<definition term="Ultimate Strength">The **maximum stress** a material can withstand before **breaking**.</definition>

<callout type="note">This point represents the **peak** of the stress-strain curve.</callout>

<callout type="note">Ultimate strength is often used to determine the **safety limits **of a material.</callout>

#### 4. Fracture Point


<definition term="Fracture Point">The point where a material **breaks** after reaching its **stress and strain limits**, beyond **elastic and plastic deformation**.</definition>

<callout type="note">Fracture occurs after the material has undergone significant **plastic deformation**.</callout>

### Material Failure: Understanding the Limits


When materials are pushed beyond their limits, they fail in different ways:

1. **Brittle Failure** – Snaps suddenly with no stretch. Examples: glass, ceramics
2. **Ductile Failure** – Stretches and deforms before breaking. Examples: steel, aluminium
3. **Fatigue Failure** – Cracks from repeated stress over time. Examples: plane wings, bridges, bike frames
